commonIssues: European (Vitis vinifera) grapes are the least cold-hardy grape type; midwinter dormant bud injury occurs roughly between -22 C and -26 C, so they suit warmer zones (about USDA 6-9) or need winter protection in colder regions.
Requires a sturdy trellis, wires, fence or arbor; without support the vine trails along the ground.
Young spring shoots after bud break are very susceptible to late-frost injury, which reduces yield.
Roots dislike sitting in wet or poorly drained soil; plant in well-drained ground and avoid overwatering.
Best grown in the ground; the listed container minimum (about 21 L / a 30 cm wide-and-deep pot per RHS) supports a single trained vine but full long-term vigor and yield favor in-ground planting.
Needs annual dormant-season pruning (before leaves emerge) to control vigor and fruit well.
Stop feeding once grapes begin to ripen and colour up, as extra feeding then can spoil flavour.

source.citation: UMN Extension - Growing grapes in the home garden (full sun; young vines need about 1/2 to 1 inch of water per week for the first two years; ~6 ft spacing per vine; self-pollinating, wind/insect pollinated; annual spring pruning; trellis/support required; spring compost feeding).
UMN Extension - Growing grapes in the home garden (vines are self-pollinating; flowers pollinated by wind and insects).

OSU Extension EC 1639 - Growing table grapes (full sunlight required for good table-grape production; European cultivars planted 6 ft apart in rows; trellis with training wires required; grow best in well-drained soils; young spring shoots very susceptible to frost injury after bud break).
OSU Extension EC 1639 - Growing table grapes (grapevines grow best in well-drained soils; roots do not tolerate waterlogged conditions).
OSU Extension EC 1639 - Growing table grapes (trellis required; young spring shoots very susceptible to frost injury after April/May bud break).

RHS - How to grow grapes / Grapes: outdoor cultivation (containers: pot about 30-38 cm (12-15 in) in diameter and depth, loam-based John Innes No3 compost; warm sheltered sunny site; almost all grape varieties self-fertile, mostly wind-pollinated; feed dessert grapes every 2-3 weeks with a high-potassium liquid feed such as tomato feed from a month after spring growth until grapes ripen, then stop; fairly drought tolerant; trained along horizontal wires).

RHS - Grapes: outdoor cultivation (feed every 2-3 weeks with a high-potassium feed such as tomato feed from ~1 month after spring growth starts until grapes ripen; stop feeding once grapes colour up).
RHS - How to grow grapes (vines are fairly drought tolerant; container vines need regular watering during the growing season; established in-ground vines often need no supplemental water).

Old Farmer's Almanac - Grapes (full sun; young grapes require about 1/2 to 1 inch of water per week; each vine needs about 6 ft of space; vines need a trellis/arbor support; European grapes do well in warm, dry Mediterranean-type zones).

OSU/Cornell-cited grapevine cold-hardiness data: maximum midwinter cold hardiness of common Vitis vinifera buds ranges roughly -14.3 F to -7.4 F (about -25.7 C to -21.9 C); V. vinifera is the least cold-hardy grape type. coldHardyMinC set conservatively to -23 C; suited to roughly USDA zones 6-9.

Confidence note: heatStressC (38 C), hotWeatherMultiplier, rainSkipThresholdMm, container liter conversions, and exact dormancy/productive months are estimated. Sources give qualitative guidance (full sun, drought tolerant once established, Mediterranean-adapted, pot 30-38 cm, 1/2-1 inch/week young vines) but not exact daily watering intervals, heat-stress thresholds, or month windows; these were converted/estimated conservatively from the cited care text. Productive/dormancy months are Northern-Hemisphere temperate-climate generalizations and shift by region and cultivar.
